concept_name: Motion of Charge in Electric Field
locked_facts: Force on charge in electric field: F = qE
Acceleration: a = qE/m (constant in uniform field)
Positive charge accelerates in direction of E, negative charge opposite to E
Charge entering perpendicular to uniform field follows a parabolic path (like projectile motion)
Horizontal velocity remains constant (no force component along it)
Vertical displacement: y = ½at² = qEL²/(2mv²) where L is length of field region
After leaving the field, the charge moves in a straight line (no force)
Analogous to projectile motion with g replaced by qE/m
truth_statements: A charged particle in a uniform electric field experiences a constant force and hence a constant acceleration
The path of a charge entering perpendicular to a uniform electric field is parabolic, exactly like projectile motion under gravity
The deflection of a charged particle is proportional to the field strength and inversely proportional to the kinetic energy
An electron and a proton in the same field experience the same force magnitude but very different accelerations due to their mass difference
This principle is used in CRT displays and ink-jet printers to deflect charged particles

aha_moment.what_to_show: An electron and a proton entering the same uniform field side by side — both experience the same force F = eE but the electron curves sharply (small mass, huge acceleration) while the proton barely deflects (large mass, small acceleration). The parabolic paths are clearly different.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two parabolic trajectories overlaid — electron's tight curve vs proton's gentle curve — with acceleration values displayed
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students assume same charge means same deflection — the mass ratio makes the trajectories dramatically different

simulation_states.label: Charge in uniform field
simulation_states.description: A uniform electric field E between parallel plates. A positive charge is placed at rest. Force arrow F = qE shown. The charge accelerates along E.
simulation_states.emphasis: F = qE gives constant acceleration in uniform field

simulation_states.label: Parabolic path
simulation_states.description: Charge enters perpendicular to the field with horizontal velocity v. It traces a parabolic path — horizontal motion uniform, vertical motion accelerated. Compared to projectile motion.
simulation_states.emphasis: Horizontal: constant velocity. Vertical: constant acceleration. Result: parabola.

simulation_states.label: Electron vs proton
simulation_states.description: Both an electron and proton enter the field. Same |F| but electron has 1836× more acceleration. Electron curves sharply, proton barely deflects. Acceleration values displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same force, vastly different masses → vastly different trajectories

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts field strength, entry velocity, and particle type. Trajectory updates in real time. Deflection angle and exit velocity calculated. Graph shows y vs x (parabola).
simulation_states.emphasis: Increase E → more deflection. Increase v → less deflection. Try different particles.

common_misconceptions.belief: The charge follows the electric field lines
common_misconceptions.correction: The charge follows field lines only if it starts from rest. If it enters with a velocity perpendicular to E, the path is parabolic — not along field lines. The field gives the direction of acceleration, not velocity.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show charge entering perpendicular to E — path clearly different from field line direction

common_misconceptions.belief: Heavier particles deflect more because they have more charge
common_misconceptions.correction: For particles with the same charge (like electron and proton), the lighter particle deflects MORE because a = F/m. The electron, being ~1836 times lighter, has ~1836 times more acceleration.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Side-by-side electron and proton trajectories showing dramatically different curvature
